ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried chicken food product class; no specific Korean soy sauce fried chicken entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FAN covers fried chicken meat generically; no specific Korean soy sauce variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Korean soy sauce fried chicken. SNOMED CT does not enumerate specific ethnic fried chicken preparations at this level of specificity.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately half a portion of a shared platter)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Korean boneless soy sauce fried chicken is approximately 250 g, representing roughly half of a shared restaurant portion of around 500 g total.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken, coated, from fast food or restaurant — adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy item 05062 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, fried) and USDA Branded Food entries for Korean-style fried chicken with soy sauce glaze
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Korean boneless soy sauce fried chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (coated, meat and skin) entries and adjusted for the soy-honey glaze contribution to carbohydrate and sodium. Energy, protein, and fat are anchored to USDA FDC item 05062 and comparable fried chicken coated entries. Sodium is elevated to reflect soy sauce marinade. Carbohydrate and sugar are elevated to reflect honey-soy glaze. This is an estimated composition.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fried chicken and battered/coated meat products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for battered and fried foods with sweet glaze
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean soy sauce fried chicken. GI is estimated at 50 based on analogous battered fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 40–55 for coated fried chicken products. The honey-soy glaze adds simple sugars but the dominant carbohydrate source remains the wheat flour batter, which moderates the overall GI. GL per serving calculated as (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 x 32.5) / 100 = 16.3, based on 250 g serving containing approximately 32.5 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce marinade and glaze
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content of approximately 620 mg per 100 g (approximately 1550 mg per 250 g serving), which represents over 65% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from honey-soy glaze and wheat flour batter combined with high fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in small portions as the honey-soy glaze contributes approximately 6.5 g of sugar per 100 g and the estimated glycemic load per standard serving is 16.3, which is in the moderate range and may cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium from deep-frying and soy sauce glaze
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its combination of saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), cholesterol (78 mg/100 g), and high sodium (620 mg/100 g), all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boneless Soya Sauce Fried Chicken is a Korean-style fried chicken dish in which boneless chicken pieces are coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried until crispy, then tossed or glazed in a sweet-savoury sauce made from soy sauce (ganjang), honey, garlic, and ginger. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior and juicy interior, carrying a rich umami flavour balanced by sweetness from the honey glaze. In South Korea, this style of fried chicken (chikin) is a popular street food and restaurant staple, often served with beer (chimaek culture) and pickled radish. In Singapore, it is served at Korean fried chicken restaurants such as O Chicken and Beer. Nutritionally, a 250 g serving provides approximately 663 kcal, 51 g protein, 36 g fat, and 33 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (approximately 1550 mg per serving) due to the soy sauce marinade and glaze, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 16.3, relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish contains gluten (wheat flour batter) and soy allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It is generally considered halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken and soy sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Korean soy sauce fried chicken?
Is soy sauce fried chicken suitable for diabetics?
Honey soy fried chicken calories Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Korean boneless soy sauce fried chicken was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (coated) entries with adjustments for the soy-honey glaze. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for this adaptation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entries. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the adapted USDA source. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken coated entries and adjusted for the soy-honey glaze contribution; this record should be updated if an exact laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo entry becomes available. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low per the Veritas threshold rule (GI <= 55 = low). GL per serving = (50 x 32.5) / 100 = 16.3 based on 250 g serving.
